sentences.text: Then, OK, she stayed-- she was in city.
[INAUDIBLE] not be there because they go slowly, slowly.
They started-- they have to go in.
They make all-- all the time less people, less people.
More and more until they get finished.
After all, long time, she was-- they left her.
You cannot kick her out because my uncle works for them, and I work for my uncle.
He said, if we don't work, then take [INAUDIBLE].
I said, I'm going with my mother.
Then open-- they make it Judenrein.
Then in the night, they knocked on the door.
Everybody's to go now-- on the [INAUDIBLE] next to the temple, so the shul.
My mother went to get my sister.
My mother said, take-- take-- [CRYING] take away.
I said, they can't do nothing.
Then they take to my mother, [INAUDIBLE] take much, take [?
Then the Gestapo take a gun, a rifle, and knocked her over the head like this.
For this time, I cannot stay no more in the house.
Then-- OK, then they took-- they took her away.
They got-- well, then they took her back to work.
A little but not much people left over to work for them.
Then I went to my-- my girlfriend, my girlfriend.
And I stayed with-- with her parents.
Then they used to took for me [INAUDIBLE], to work-- to work.
I used to stay with them.
After all, [? this is not ?] nothing.
The Germans, they go like this, slowly, slowly, until they liquidated the whole thing.
Then last time, they said, the city got to be a Judenrein.
I hiding with my [INAUDIBLE], my brother and [INAUDIBLE] in their basement.
Midnight, used to go around the Jewish [INAUDIBLE].
When they find over there, if you're hiding, [INAUDIBLE] nothing.
The Jews, everybody's going to be Judenrein.
No more Jews in this city.
And if they see you, they're gonna kill you anyway.
My uncle-- my uncle was-- and he worked for them.
They didn't pay him back to take him to gas chamber.
They don't take him to gas chamber.
Before, I'm going to tell you later about this.
They took us on a way of there from-- from our city to [?
Germans. ?] The Germans let us [? go. ?] The Germans watch us.
The Germans that was going with us, the Gestapo.
Let's go to-- they said-- they said, before you go to the gas chamber, they take you there.
They take you [? over ?] [? to ?] the ghetto.
Now, way back over there, the Jews-- a lot the Jews are lost for now.
You know, people-- you know-- not-- [INAUDIBLE], the people used to work for-- I don't know how to say it here-- like truck driver-- truck driver, other things.
They want to make a attack of the Germans.
The-- the women start to cry.
Because they want to do it.
They run away to fields in [? partisan. ?] They said, no.
And then [INAUDIBLE], put us in the ghetto.
You have to [? see ?] all the time.
Then we started to make a-- [INAUDIBLE] how you call a hiding place, [YIDDISH] in Yiddish.
How you do it-- from the wall, you make a extra wall and then pull out long-- long board, and put-- you go in and covered.
With my cousin-- oh, with my cousin, with my-- and them.
They-- the-- the father died before the war.
With her mother and brothers and [INAUDIBLE], all of them.
They were hiding together with us and-- and other people.
Then somebody told them about us.
They took him out, and they know [? where ?] and we're still alive.
The Gestapo with their guns like this.
[INAUDIBLE] center of the city, sitting on their knees, everybody.
Everybody sitting on their knees from the morning, almost all day, sitting on the [INAUDIBLE] over there.
Then he goes around, around, and look what to do with them.
Which one to take out right away and which one to take some later.
Then they took out my uncle, [?
[CRYING] They said to him, we not going to send you to Treblinka.
We can-- we finish you here.
And they kill him right away.
Then I sitting next to my-- [INAUDIBLE] a girl from [INAUDIBLE].
They shoot her right away next to me.
I was lucky they didn't shoot me.
They decide to take us all over in-- in the cemetery because they'd rather dig the ditches to bury-- to bury us.
I went to the cemetery in [INAUDIBLE], going with him.
You go behind us, Gestapo with guns and [INAUDIBLE] with their rifles.
After all, the [INAUDIBLE] order to take us back out.
Then they took us on the train.
Train-- cattle train-- not a real train, cattle train.
Close up, locked up, and then they took us over there.
Takes about two days to take us around with no water or nothing.
And I was myself running next to a little-- little opening to get air-- and can still breathe.
Then they took us to Majdanek.
Oh, I don't know how many people.
I don't know how many people.
I don't know how to count even.
My-- my brain doesn't work for this no more.
Oh, at this time, I was about around-- about 20 years old, 21.
Because the [INAUDIBLE] was, they [? didn't ?] took me to war.
The Polish because-- Poland-- eight days was-- was-- was lost the war.
If they had more time, they would've taken me too.
They got no time for this to take me to war.
Polish-- you know, Polish people was-- how you call it?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sell the Poland to the [INAUDIBLE].
The-- the Germans airplanes was flying.
They shot-- they shot [? them ?] [INAUDIBLE] where to [? bomber. ?] A lot of them no good.
I mean, Poland was no good.
Polish people were spies against them own self.
Do you remember what year this was when they took you to Majdanek?
I think in 1942 because I got written-- I check myself.
I don't have to [INAUDIBLE], maybe before this.
I think I lost my mind.
I cannot sleep in the night.
Sometimes I think they run after me in the night.
Well, I said, [INAUDIBLE] my wife, what's going to you?
Then in Majdanek, come to Majdanek, was a sign.
And I said, can I get a drink?
You can go into gas chamber.
But [INAUDIBLE] was a sign over there.
The work in Majdanek was not work over there.
They take me with another man, take a board on my-- my shoulder, the other-- the other side from the other shoulder and put on rocks and this.
They run after this with-- with a-- with a stick, a rubber stick like this, knocked [INAUDIBLE].
Then they took us-- one time took us out from there, took us out from the Majdanek to work.
I forgot the name of it.
I was over there to clean up this place.
Everything was [? hot even. ?] Children's-- children's bed, everything, and food-- [INAUDIBLE].
They took them away to Majdanek, to burn.
[INAUDIBLE], Majdanek was a-- the-- the-- the women's place, another barrack, another place.
I used to go to the wires and talk.
In Majdanek, they give us work.
You're never going to believe that kind of work.
We used to clean off the toilets, put it over [? one, ?] and clean the [INAUDIBLE] on the fields.
And I used to go and do it like this to make it soft, the ground, from the toilets with these men.
They took us-- I-- I figured they going to take us right away-- oh, I forget-- they took us clean up, put in-- put in a pool something.
I figured they were-- before this, I figure they gonna take us to gas chamber.
They took a [INAUDIBLE], my cousin away, everybody.
Children-- I had my two cousins, little 10 years, 12 years, never seen [? work. ?] Took here.
We never know who got to die, who got to live.
I didn't know where I'm going.
Even Majdanek, every night they took down people.
They don't give them to eat nothing.
They took all the time appel [INAUDIBLE] on the fields.
They count the people if they are still there.
They know that we never can run.
They put on somebody-- if-- I cannot explain everything.
So when you first arrived, they gave you like a disinfectant bath.
Take you right away and give you a place to stay, and that's it.
Oh, one over the top from the others.
This a barrack like for horses.
They give you just in the morning, and that's it.
If you don't eat this, you got nothing.
Sometime I go in the night, I come down.
You have to go down sometime.
And I said to my cousin-- he was my cousin, the picture you got, [INAUDIBLE] for them.
He's [? laying on me. ?] I said, [INAUDIBLE].
I come up, no more bread.
I never smoke, and I needed bread.
Even my cousin, you know, [? never care. ?] [INAUDIBLE] don't care [? if he's a ?] cousin [INAUDIBLE].
Later the next day, take out a piece of bread.
And little piece of bread all day.
Who was watching over you in these barracks?
Over you, they had the kapos.
Even I used to go out-- the Jewish [INAUDIBLE] leader, some they give out little water, like a soup.
I don't what this even was.
Maybe it was in the bottom [INAUDIBLE] a little better, even from the top.
I said to him, why you [INAUDIBLE]?
You know, people was in the war, even you can be the closest to you, was terrible.
We'll come to this later on.
Yeah, I was not very long because later on, they were looking for younger people to work, out-- out to work-- to take them to work.
Put them on the-- put them on a wagon.
Put them on a wagon, right away [? from ?] the wagon.
They kill them already on the-- on fields-- how you call it?
They call it like, like the soldiers stays and count them.
[INAUDIBLE] come, they take the [? tents ?] out to kill.
They did daily all the time, every time.
Put them on the wagon right away to burn them.
You think I'm going-- it's not just [INAUDIBLE].
You couldn't fathom what I think.
Then from this, they-- they started to take out-- take out to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us out, [INAUDIBLE] to the Germans, Gestapo, looking who was good, take off everything, the clothes.
Then they go around to see how you look.
My cousin was a little-- actually and before the war-- one foot was a little shorter.
[INAUDIBLE] limped onto one foot, this side.
Then they figure I'm a good slave for work.
They took-- took a-- put everybody in a line and see.
The [? tent ?] [INAUDIBLE], and I look from the side.
I'm afraid I'm in the [? tent, ?] and they're going to get me out for killing for nothing.
I started to take my shoes, my shoes-- tie my shoes, [? whether ?] they are together.
Then I go on the other side.
If I be the [? tent, ?] they come to [? the tent-- ?] the [? tent-- ?] it out, I'll be dead.
My cousin, he took my bread.
All day, more than a day.
Oh, I don't-- I don't know it.
I'm in the [? tent. ?] I was afraid, and I did it.
Then when-- then when they took to checking who's got good for work, I went out, they took me to work.
From this time on, they send-- they send out from Majdanek.
They gave us a bread on the way to go there.
I don't know where they send us.
They send us to-- to Skarzysko.
There-- over there was a factory, ammunition factory.
I go with-- with the truth.
Sometime the truth's not good to tell.
I think I can work here.
Maybe they need it for a tailor.
Was two camps over there-- A, B, C.
First of all, [INAUDIBLE] first time, I went over there.
Give you [? to dress. ?] Over there, it was more-- [? it's in ?] the camp.
Over there the leaders was Jews.
You never-- you think I'm gonna count the barracks?
I never count in my life.
I cannot count-- even- I cannot count barracks.
I was not sure of my life tomorrow.
What-- what interested me the barracks if I can't do nothing?
If I [INAUDIBLE], I [? figure, ?] you know, [INAUDIBLE] the people.
You cannot go out of this.
Like-- was like here in a jail-- worse.
They took you out from there for-- for labor.
The work in the-- they don't need the labor.
Just to beat you up, all the time.
Sometimes we [? can ?] appel to go outside, and they said, run!
Not in this line, not in Majdanek.
In other-- other camps had it different.
When you left Majdanek, how did you get to Skarzysko?
I don't know how I get in train.
Oh, they-- they-- going from Majdanek to Skarzysko, they give you bread.
They treat you right on the way.
[INAUDIBLE] where you going because they need you for work over there.
Hell-- hell, better than this work, what I got.
First of all-- first time they took me to Skarzysko, the work not so bad.
I used to work in a factory for ammunition.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE] ammunition, put on-- I don't know you call this-- [INAUDIBLE] ammunition [INAUDIBLE] to make like a-- oh boy.
Take-- I don't know how to explain this, how you call this.
All kind of work, and I did it right.
I can do it-- [INAUDIBLE] I did right.
The only thing, later on, the people-- a lot the people work with transport-- [INAUDIBLE] trains, put the ammunition on the trains to the war.
Some people work [INAUDIBLE], some other work in factory was some [INAUDIBLE] like green stuff.
[INAUDIBLE] they die fast, they take-- they take people from the easy work back to this.
That's-- then one night, I see-- working over there in the factory, I see camps of Gestapo in the night.
The first night, they took me to [?
This is what happened-- because after my mind comes back to tell you the truth, because I don't know what's happen.
In the night, they come over there, looking for other people out for the hard labor on the trains.
The people that were working with me was from Lodz.
They know me-- all girls know me.
[INAUDIBLE] [? hiding ?] [? you. ?] They can't find us.
They pick me because I was young, looking good.
They pick me to the work.
The same night, they took me to the-- to working, and I wait from this place.
This work was-- the name from this factory was [?
Over there, at-- at the front-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a policeman, a Jewish policeman, [INAUDIBLE].
He was very good to me.
He asked me to [INAUDIBLE] because I did work for him.
And at night, he give me something more, more food to eat and [INAUDIBLE].
Then I did-- when they kick me out, they seem right.
They transport-- I'm not just a transport.
My hand was for tailor, not-- cannot carry-- was bullets like this for the-- for the tanks.
The Polish people-- Polish-- he knocked me almost to death in night.
And after all, I started to talk to him.
Kravits in Polish-- you know a kravits is a tailor-- a tailor.
I can do it for you.
I started to soft him down.
And this was the last [INAUDIBLE] over there.
Just in paper, I did my-- my leg like this.
Mother, ?] I don't want to live no more.
Work in the night of transport and cold.
In the night, it frost in Poland.
I can't stand it no more.
Where can I go to die or-- or to do it?
One time-- I don't know when this is happening-- then they took me after this-- I have to push a [INAUDIBLE], transport, log on the transport from the-- from the train.
I have to push the wagons-- another put people.
And then behind you, a Gestapo, and he knocks you to death.
I was too mad to run away.
And then he run after me-- he run after me.
I run to the factory, the Jewish working there, through the factory, run out the factory.
And they ask the factory, you see this-- they didn't know me everybody.
He said, we don't know if seen nobody.
I sleep all night in toilet, and I was afraid tomorrow to go to-- to go back to the-- the camp.
I was afraid that he did it.
I cannot stand it no more.
They never going to [? kill him. ?] Everybody knows me over in the lager.
Because I did good, very [? bad. ?] [INAUDIBLE] I do good.
The same thing then what's happened.
My children don't like for me to tell them this.
This-- there I make it from that.
I walk-- I walk over there until I-- until they take me away from there.
One time-- and over there, in the same camp was a lot of people not far from our city-- [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], name it.
I used to go over there to barber.
Some people, they don't like it.
People who come from Majdanek to this later, they don't like it.
They call the Majdanek, they call them with names.
They don't call me because I don't know.
I did for the police Jewish [? woman ?] sometime.
And sometime, I was sitting-- they took me in a Jewish-- in-- in jail, in camp.
And he comes-- the Gestapo comes, and you're sitting here because you don't want to go to work.
They look on, and I was hiding under the [? bridge ?] in the bathroom.
And [? go ?] me with a cane to see if they can catch me.
I see the cane going there, I go and run on the other side.
Then they find me out, and they put [? me ?] over there in the jail.
I don't know what this is.
And the Gestapo comes later, and takes off in the-- that-- they take you of a place and shoot you and good-bye.
And then, I know from-- from the-- the-- land leader, was-- he tooks care of the people, that are sick.
I know [? him's wife. ?] I know him.
I said-- I holler-- I holler through the window and said, please, go tell this man I'm-- I'm in jail.
If they didn't took me out this day, I would be dead.
He was a leader from-- from Skarzysko too.
He-- he was-- oh, he was good, always good to me.
Now, [INAUDIBLE] people-- people are going to a [INAUDIBLE] in Landsberg, in Skarzysko.
They [INAUDIBLE] because they did-- did something.
One time they took me-- they took us every Sunday from [INAUDIBLE] to [INAUDIBLE].
They take a bet over there because they [INAUDIBLE] it was nothing, except they take [? the worker. ?]
I was a tailor, but I need thread.
And also I got a little scissors, nail scissors.
They have cases of it, that they used to be packed, the ammunition.
They cut it off, and this is sabotage.
You know what I mean sabotage?
I know in English it's sabotage.
I bought it for something else.
Then they knew, and cut it.
Then before-- before, we have to go back to [INAUDIBLE].
They-- they check everybody, and my heart was like this, pumped like this.
I was afraid because I was-- it hiding places, and they cannot find me.
[INAUDIBLE] came right [? of ?] the place, right [? on ?] place.
I fight for life to be alive.
We're gonna stop now, and we'll change the tape.
You're gonna use so many tapes.
